features.popup:
Sewee Shell Ring (38CH45)

Late Archaic (~4,000 BP / ~2200-1000 BCE) — Ancestral Thom's Creek culture

Location: Awendaw, Charleston County (32.969°N, 79.710°W)

Northernmost shell ring in North America. C-shaped ring ~150 ft diameter, walls 10 ft high, composed primarily of oyster shells with mussels, clams, and periwinkles. Built ~4,000 years ago — contemporary with the Great Pyramids of Giza and Stonehenge. Contains pottery sherds (some of earliest in North America), stone tools, bone fragments including dog remains (early evidence of dog domestication). Human remains with evidence of cutting/burning found. Shell rings exist in only four places on Earth: SE United States, Japan, Colombia, and Peru. Nearly half destroyed in 1920s by road crews mining material for Highway 17 paving. Now protected in Francis Marion National Forest with 1-mile interpretive trail and boardwalk. NRHP listed 1970.

Water-storage hypothesis: Marquardt (2010) and Middaugh (2009) proposed that the Sewee Shell Ring functioned primarily as a freshwater reservoir. Hydrological testing found the ring still accumulates potable freshwater today — even after 3,000+ years of sediment infilling. Three distinct berms and a slough shell structure functioned as weirs to control water flow. The interior 'plaza' contained clean sand with almost no shell, consistent with a water collection basin. The ring (~75 m diameter, ~2,900 cubic meters of shell) was built during the Mid-Holocene Cool Period (~5000-3500 BP), when sea level was 0.8-3.0 m lower and coastal freshwater was scarce. Freshwater springs or sloughs are found near many other SC rings, suggesting strategic placement for access to both marine food and potable water. This challenges dominant 'feasting monument' theories and reframes the rings as sophisticated paleo-engineering.

Visitor info: Francis Marion National Forest, Sewee Shell Ring Interpretive Trail — free, open dawn to dusk.

Sources: Trinkley 1985; Russo & Heide 2003; Hemmings 1970; SAH Archipedia; Marquardt 2010 (American Antiquity 75:551-570); Middaugh 2009 (Journal of the NC Academy of Science)

Fig Island Shell Ring Complex (38CH42)

National Historic Landmark — Late Archaic (~4,400-3,600 BP)

Location: Edisto Island, Charleston County (32.571°N, 80.214°W)

Most complex shell ring site in North America. Three rings on a peninsula extending from Edisto Island along the North Edisto River. Ring 1: ~600 ft long, 20-25 ft above sea level, with four attached smaller rings. Ring 2: circular, 215 ft diameter. Ring 3: smaller ring. Contains some of the earliest pottery known in North America. Excavations revealed deliberate rapid deposition of shells — monumental construction, not refuse accumulation. By total volume, the largest single Archaic shell ring complex on US coasts. State-owned (Botany Bay Plantation, SC Dept of Natural Resources). Sea level was ~4 ft lower when built. NRHP listed 1970; NHL designated.

Sources: Russo & Saunders 2004; NPS NHL nomination; SCDAH

Lighthouse Point Shell Ring (38CH12)

Late Archaic / Early Woodland — James Island

Location: James Island, Charleston County (32.735°N, 79.960°W)

Also called Parrot's Point Shell Ring. Once a complete circle ~240 paces across and 8-10 ft high (Governor John Drayton, 1802). Contains the most diverse faunal assemblage of any shell ring — catfish, drum, mullet, bowfin, rays, toads, snakes, turtles, turkey, clapper rail, ducks, perching birds, rabbit, rat, squirrel, opossum, dog, raccoon, mink, and deer. Human remains found as bone fragments and coprolites. Heavily disturbed by erosion and shell mining. NRHP listed 1990.

Sources: Trinkley 1980a; Drayton 1802; SCDAH

Horse Island Shell Ring (38CH14)

Late Archaic

Location: Near Rockville/Wadmalaw, Charleston County (32.618°N, 80.185°W)

Late Archaic shell ring near the Rockville/Wadmalaw area. Part of the dense cluster of shell rings along the South Carolina Sea Islands. Limited published excavation data. NRHP listed.

Sources: NPS Archaic Shell Rings context study

Hanckel Mound / Leadenwah Creek Shell Ring (38CH7)

Late Archaic

Location: Near Rockville, Leadenwah Creek, Charleston County (32.606°N, 80.228°W)

Also called Leadenwah Creek Mount. ~158 ft diameter, 8 ft high. One of ~20 shell rings identified along the SC coastal plain, all located along tidal creeks. Composed primarily of oyster shell. NRHP listed 1970.

Sources: SCDAH; NPS Archaic Shell Rings context study

Stratton Place Shell Ring (38CH24)

Late Archaic — Johns Island/Edisto area

Location: Johns Island area, Charleston County (32.650°N, 80.170°W)

Late Archaic shell ring in the Johns Island/Edisto area. Archaeological evidence shows houses and hearths during early ring occupation that disappear in later stages — suggesting a transition from residential use to ceremonial/monumental function over time. Part of the Charleston County cluster of shell rings.

Sources: NPS Archaic Shell Rings context study; Russo 2004

Charles Towne Landing — Mississippian Ceremonial Center

Cusabo (Kiawah, Etiwan); earlier Woodland and Mississippian peoples

Location: Albemarle Point, Ashley River, Charleston (32.807°N, 79.988°W)

Date: 6,000+ years continuous occupation — Woodland through Mississippian through Contact

One of the most layered archaeological sites in coastal South Carolina. Contains Woodland era shell middens, a Mississippian ceremonial center, and evidence of Native American villages, campsites, and a ceremonial center spanning 6,000+ years. The Cassique (chief) of the Kiawah directed English colonists to settle here in 1670. Ashley series pottery — the diagnostic Cusabo ceramic type — was first identified here by Stanley South in the late 1960s. Also yielded Creek trade pottery from central Alabama, demonstrating long-distance exchange networks. Active archaeological excavations ongoing. Now Charles Towne Landing State Historic Site (664 acres). Contains a Native American cemetery and ceremonial center on park grounds.

Visitor info: Charles Towne Landing State Historic Site, 1500 Old Towne Rd, Charleston — open daily.

Sources: South 1969; Zierden (Charleston Museum); Isenbarger (CTL Archaeologist); SCIAA; Marcoux, Nyman, Cable (Ashley series)

Buck Hall Burial Mound (38CH644)

Middle Woodland — Sewee (ancestral); coastal Woodland peoples

Location: Francis Marion National Forest, near Awendaw Creek, Charleston County (33.038°N, 79.548°W)

Middle Woodland sand burial mound documenting the extension of North Carolina coastal burial mound traditions southward into Charleston County. Contains ossuaries (collective bone deposits) with Deptford and Deep Creek ceramic wares. Excavated by Michael Trinkley in February 1983. Significant for demonstrating that sand burial mounds — previously thought limited to the NC coast — were a more common practice among coastal Woodland groups than previously recognized.

Sources: Trinkley 1991, Chicora Foundation Research Series 25

Dill Sanctuary (James Island)

Cusabo — Woodland through Contact period

Location: James Island, Charleston County (32.728°N, 79.945°W)

Multi-period archaeological site on James Island, excavated by the Charleston Museum. Yielded both local Ashley series Cusabo pottery and Creek pottery from central Alabama — demonstrating long-distance trade/exchange networks connecting coastal South Carolina with peoples hundreds of miles to the southwest. Also produced colonoware (low-fired earthenware made in the Lowcountry through the 18th century). One of several sites contributing to the identification and naming of the Ashley ceramic series.

Sources: Zierden (Charleston Museum); Marcoux, Nyman, Cable; Jon Marcoux

Santee Indian Mound / Fort Watson

Santee — Mississippian (~1200-1500 CE)

Location: Santee National Wildlife Refuge, Clarendon County (33.539°N, 80.438°W)

Date: Mississippian ~1200-1500 CE; Revolutionary War fort 1780-1781

Mississippian burial and/or temple mound, part of a Santee mound village complex on the Santee River (now Lake Marion). The Santee held elaborate burial rituals — chiefs, shamans, and warriors were interred on mounds with wooden pole structures and offerings of rattles and feathers. Mound height indicated the deceased's importance. Common people were wrapped in bark on platforms; relatives painted faces black and kept vigil. Bones were later cleaned, boxed, and re-oiled annually. At least 16 graves excavated by archaeologists. During the American Revolution, the British built Fort Watson (30-50 ft high) atop the mound. In 1781, Francis Marion and Light Horse Harry Lee captured it by building a log tower in a single night — the first British outpost recaptured after the 1780 occupation. No remains of the fort survive. Now in Santee National Wildlife Refuge. NRHP listed 1969.

Visitor info: Santee National Wildlife Refuge — free, open during refuge hours. Fort Watson site has interpretive markers.

Sources: SCDAH; NPS; American Battlefield Trust

Pockoy Island Shell Ring Complex

HERITAGE AT RISK — Late Archaic (~4,300 BP) — Thom's Creek culture

Location: Botany Bay Heritage Preserve, Edisto Island, Charleston County (32.498°N, 80.225°W)

Two Late Archaic shell rings discovered via LiDAR scanning in late 2016 after Hurricane Matthew. Pockoy 1 was donut-shaped, ~60 meters across, constructed and occupied over a very short period (20-100 years). Contained some of the oldest ceramics in the United States — Thom's Creek pottery with sand tempering and bold punctated designs, as well as beautifully decorated deer bone pins incised with designs that may have been tribal identifiers handed down for generations. Also found Stallings Island pottery (one of the earliest pottery traditions in North America, using Spanish moss as temper).

Heritage at Risk: Pockoy 1 has been completely lost to coastal erosion — the island was eroding at ~50 feet per year. It was one of the most intensively investigated shell rings in the world before the ocean claimed it. Ring 2 was expected to follow within a few years. Over 13,000 archaeological sites along the south Atlantic and Gulf coasts are potentially at risk with just 3 feet of sea level rise.

Sources: SCDNR Heritage Trust Program; Gaillard & Smith; Cochran (East Tennessee State); Garcia 2019 (SC Wild); Anderson et al. 2017

Spanish Mount Point (38CH62)

HERITAGE AT RISK — Late Archaic (~2200-1800 BCE) — Thom's Creek culture

Location: Edisto Beach State Park, Scott Creek, Charleston County (32.507°N, 80.323°W)

Also called the 'Indian Mound' locally. An oyster-clam shell midden approximately 27 meters long and 3 meters high, bordered by water and marsh on three sides along Scott Creek. Possibly a shell ring that was later infilled with shell over time. Archaeological finds include Thom's Creek pottery, Stallings Island pottery (using Spanish moss as temper), decorated deer bone pins used as hair accessories, soapstone vessels for indirect heating of water and food, and abundant faunal remains. Soapstone came from interior Piedmont quarries, indicating long-distance trade. Excavated by Donald Sutherland in 1973-1974 for SCIAA.

Heritage at Risk: A retaining wall installed in the 2000s to combat erosion was ruptured by the 2015 floods. Most of Spanish Mount has now been overwashed by tidal creek action.

Visitor info: Accessible via 1.7-mile trail through maritime forest. NRHP listed 1974.

Sources: Sutherland 1974 (South Carolina Antiquities vol. 6); Chamberlain 1980; New South Associates 1992; SCDAH

Edisto Beach State Park Shell Middens

Cusabo (Edisto subtribe); earlier Archaic peoples — Late Archaic through Contact

Location: Edisto Beach State Park, Colleton County (32.503°N, 80.315°W)

At least 16 Late Archaic archaeological components identified in a 1992 cultural resource management survey by New South Associates. The island itself is named for the Edistow (Edisto) people, a Cusabo subtribe who originally lived south of the Port Royal River but moved to Edisto Island after the Escamacu War of 1576-1579. Continuous evidence of human occupation from the Late Archaic through the Contact period, including shell middens, lithic scatters, and ceramic deposits. The state-recognized Edisto Natchez-Kusso Tribe (recognized 2008, 784 members documented in 2001) and the Wassamasaw people trace their lineage to the Edisto and other Cusabo subtribes and claim burial grounds on Edisto Island and other Lowcountry islands. Human remains have been found eroding from the beachfront in recent years.

Sources: New South Associates 1992; Waddell 1980; SCDAH; Chicora Foundation

Sea Pines Shell Ring (38BU7)

Late Archaic (~4,000 BP) — Best-preserved shell ring in the Southeast

Location: Sea Pines Forest Preserve, Hilton Head Island, Beaufort County (32.128°N, 80.832°W)

The most pristine and best-preserved shell ring in the southeastern United States. A near-perfect circle of shells ~150 feet in diameter, built over approximately 300 years around 4,000 years ago. Composed of thousands of discarded oysters, clams, whelk, turtle, fish, and other animal remains. Originally stood 2-3 feet tall but is now mostly covered by soil, barely distinguishable from surrounding ground — which is precisely what preserved it. No signs anyone lived directly in the ring — likely a ceremonial area and community plaza. Currently being studied by Binghamton University archaeologists led by Dr. Matthew Sanger. Two other shell rings on Hilton Head were destroyed when shells were removed to make tabby for roads and buildings. NRHP listed.

Ritual reuse: Sanger et al. (2021) found that later southeastern coastal peoples engaged in ritual reuse of the Sea Pines Shell Ring more than 1,000 years after its initial construction, by cremating human and non-human bodies at the site. The findings suggest shell rings were 'persistent places' — powerful, sacred locales with a social and cosmological gravity that attracted people across many generations. The concept of 'revelatory locales' frames these sites as points where past, present, and future collapse — aligning with how many Native American communities understand significant places.

Visitor info: Sea Pines Forest Preserve — open to public with interpretive signage.

Sources: Calmes 1967; NPS shell ring context study; Sanger (Binghamton University); discoversouthcarolina.com; Sanger et al. 2021 (Journal of Anthropological Archaeology 63:101322)

Skull Creek Shell Rings (38BU8)

Late Archaic (early second millennium BCE) — Figure-8 conjoined rings

Location: Skull Creek Drive, north Hilton Head Island, Beaufort County (32.247°N, 80.748°W)

Two conjoined Late Archaic shell rings in a distinctive figure-8 configuration. The only known example in the Southeast of a later ring superimposed over an earlier one, though the precise relationship between the two has not been determined. The southernmost ring (No. 1) is approximately 128 feet in diameter and stands about 7.5 feet at some points above a flat central area. Like all shell rings in the region, composed primarily of oyster shells mixed with other mollusks, animal bones, and pottery sherds. Contains some of the earliest pottery in North America. Has suffered extensive shell removal over the years but is now NRHP listed and protected.

Sources: Calmes 1967; Heide & Russo 2003; NPS shell ring context study; SCDAH

Green's Shell Enclosure

HERITAGE AT RISK — Late Archaic through Mississippian (~4,000 BP – ~1400 CE)

Location: Squire Pope Road, near Skull Creek, Hilton Head Island, Beaufort County (32.238°N, 80.740°W)

A shell ring / enclosure now preserved as Green Shell Heritage Preserve. Currently being surveyed by SCDNR Heritage Trust archaeologists led by Meg Gaillard (the same team that excavated Pockoy Island). January 2025 excavations found Irene Phase pottery (~1300-1400 CE) — gorgeous, well-preserved sherds from a Georgia Coast archaeological phase — demonstrating that this site had a much longer occupation history than most shell rings, spanning from the Late Archaic potentially into the Mississippian period.

Heritage at Risk: Erosion is occurring at a slower rate than Pockoy (a few centimeters per year rather than feet), giving archaeologists more time — but the threat remains ongoing.

Visitor info: Public tours offered during excavation seasons. NRHP listed.

Sources: Gaillard (SCDNR); Post & Courier 2025; NPS shell ring context study

Coosaw Shell Rings (38BU2)

Late Archaic — Figure-8 conjoined rings

Location: Coosaw Island, Beaufort County (32.465°N, 80.425°W)

Two conjoined Late Archaic shell rings in a figure-8 pattern on Coosaw Island. Along with Skull Creek, one of only two known figure-8 conjoined ring sites in South Carolina. The Coosaw River and island name derive from the Cusabo subtribe 'Coosaw' or 'Kussoe/Kussah.' Three major rings are found at the Coosaw complex (similar to Fig Island and Sapelo complexes), suggesting these multi-ring sites served as regional gathering centers. Located in a remote, marshy setting typical of shell ring placement along tidal creeks.

Sources: Heide & Russo 2003; NPS shell ring context study; Calmes 1967

Cusabo Alliance Territory

Extent: ~5,000 square miles — Cooper River south to Savannah River, ~80 miles inland

Time: ~1000 BCE – AD 1751

Peoples: At least 19 politically autonomous polities — Kussoe, Edisto, Escamacu, Kiawah, Etiwan, Ashepoo, Bohicket, Combahee, Stono, Wando, Wimbee, and others

Seasonal pattern: Coast in summer (fishing, shellfish); inland river valleys in winter (deer, turkey). Villages were circular, palisaded, with council houses and central plazas.

Population: ~1,750 in 1562; 664 by 1682; fewer than 250 by 1751. Nearly 6 of every 7 Indians on the Lower Coast died of causes directly related to European contact (Waddell).

Sources: Waddell 1980; Milling 1969; Swanton 1953

Sewee Territory

Extent: Lower Santee River and coast to Ashley River divide — Berkeley County

Time: ~2000 BCE – AD 1720

Population: ~800 in 1600; 57 by 1715; declared 'utterly extirpated' by Governor Johnson in 1720

Language: Probably Catawban/Siouan — different from Cusaboan. Place names in Sewee territory are in Catawban languages. Their name survives in Sewee Bay (now Bulls Bay) and the town of Awendaw (from their village Avendaughbough).

Notable: Renowned canoe men. Tragically lost most able-bodied men in a doomed attempt to sail to England for direct trade — survivors were enslaved in the West Indies.

Sources: Lawson 1709; Waddell 1980; Swanton 1953

Santee Territory

Extent: Santee River corridor from the Sewee settlements upstream to the forks

Time: ~800 CE – AD 1743

Culture: Mississippian tradition — burial/temple mounds. The Santee chief held unusual despotic authority (rare for the region). Elaborate burial rituals with mound interment, wooden pole structures, and offerings of rattles and feathers.

Decline: Yamasee War (1715) broke their power; many enslaved and shipped to West Indies. By 1743 consolidated with Catawba, retaining distinct dialect.

Sources: Lawson 1709; Mooney 1894; Waddell 1980

Chester Field Shell Ring (38BU29)

Late Archaic (second millennium BCE)

Location: Near Laurel Bay, Beaufort County (32.435°N, 80.725°W) — address restricted

Late Archaic shell ring containing some of the earliest pottery in North America. Sassaman (2006) noted stylistic similarities between Stallings Island decorated pottery and ceramics from Chester Field, though he considered them 'distinct, albeit closely related, communities' — suggesting cultural exchange between interior Savannah River populations and coastal ring-builders 200 km away. Marquardt (2010) identified freshwater springs or sloughs in proximity, consistent with his hypothesis that ring placement was driven by access to potable water during the dry Mid-Holocene Cool Period. NRHP listed 1970.

Sources: NPS Archaic Shell Rings context study (Russo 2006); Sassaman 2006; Marquardt 2010; SCDAH NRHP 1970

Coosaw Island Shell Ring Complex (38BU1866)

Late Archaic (~3,800-3,600 BP) — 4 rings including figure-8 pair

Location: South Bluff Heritage Preserve, Coosaw Island, Beaufort County (32.467°N, 80.458°W)

One of the most complex shell ring sites in the United States. Discovered in 2000, the complex includes two conjoined rings in a figure-8 configuration (Coosaw 1 and 2, 55-60 m diameter, up to 1.73 m high) plus two additional rings (130-170 ft diameter, 3-4 ft high). The conjoined pair extends over 300 feet and rises nearly six feet. Radiocarbon dates indicate construction began ~3,800 years ago and the site was abandoned ~200 years later. Along with Fig Island and Sapelo Island (Georgia), Coosaw is one of only three known multi-ring complexes in the Southeast, suggesting these served as regional gathering centers for trade, communication, and political relations. Artifacts include fiber-tempered pottery (both Stallings and Thom's Creek types), shell tools, bone pins, bifaces, and lithic flakes. The island name derives from the Cusabo subtribe 'Coosaw' (Kussoe/Kussah). Now preserved as South Bluff Heritage Preserve (24 acres) — one of the best-preserved shell ring complexes in the state.

Visitor info: Accessible via trails from end of Friendship Lane, Coosaw Island.

Sources: SCDNR Heritage Trust; Heide & Russo 2003; NPS shell ring context study

Ford's Shell Ring (Hilton Head)

Late Archaic (~4,000 BP) — Figure-8 conjoined rings

Location: Squire Pope community, north Hilton Head Island, Beaufort County (32.240°N, 80.745°W)

Two conjoined shell rings in a figure-8 configuration. Named for site's last private owner, Henry Ford. The smaller ring was constructed approximately 300 years earlier than the larger ring, which is partially superimposed over the older one — the older ring is approximately the same age as the Sea Pines Shell Ring, and occupants of both sites undoubtedly socialized and traded with each other. The 6.8-acre wooded waterfront lot was purchased in 2003 by the Town of Hilton Head, Heritage Trust, and Beaufort County to preempt development and protect the rings. In November 2024, Beaufort County Council voted unanimously to approve construction of a passive park for public access and protection.

Sources: Archaeological Society of SC (Kiernan 2020); Post & Courier; SCDNR

Kempfer Place Shell Ring

Late Archaic

Location: Beaufort County coast (32.350°N, 80.520°W)

Late Archaic shell ring between Stratton Place and Hilton Head. Marquardt (2010) identified freshwater springs or sloughs in proximity — along with Chester Field, Sewee, Auld, Cannon's Point, St. Catherines, and Guana — supporting his hypothesis that shell rings were strategically placed to access both marine food resources and potable freshwater during the dry Mid-Holocene Cool Period. Part of the dense network of shell rings along the South Carolina Sea Islands. South Carolina has more shell rings than any other state — at least 17 known sites with 25+ individual rings.

Sources: Marquardt 2010 (American Antiquity 75:551-570); NPS Archaic Shell Rings context study

38CH1466 — Seaside Farms Woodland Shell Midden

Middle Woodland (~500 BCE – AD 1000) — Deptford / Hanover / Santee-McClellanville phases

Location: Seaside Farms tract, near Inlet Creek, Charleston County (32.813°N, 79.814°W)

National Register: Eligible — 250 × 600 ft intact midden

Intact Middle Woodland shell midden situated behind a historic earthen berm on the Seaside Farms tract. Multi-component occupation spanning Deptford through Santee/McClellanville phases. Of 87 analyzable sherds: Deptford dominates at 69% (cord marked, check stamped, simple stamped, fabric impressed), with significant Hanover (18.4%) and minor Santee/McClellanville (8%) and Wando limestone-tempered ware (2.3%). Several Deptford sherds contained fragments of unleached limestone — possibly experimental. Test Unit 1 yielded 159 lbs of oyster shell midden at 0.6 ft depth. Excellent faunal preservation includes deer bone, and water-screening recovered ethnobotanical remains. Demonstrates the importance of coastal marsh resources to Woodland groups even on poorly drained Rutlege soils — challenging the prevailing model (Brooks & Scurry 1978) that prehistoric sites cluster only on well-drained soils. Site likely functioned as a seasonal/extractive camp oriented toward shellfish collection and deer hunting.

Sources: Adams & Trinkley 1993, Chicora Foundation Research Series 35

38CH1474 — Wando Series Type Site

TYPE SITE — Woodland period — First formal description of Wando Series limestone-tempered pottery

Location: Marsh island, Seaside Farms tract, Charleston County (32.803°N, 79.818°W)

National Register: Eligible — 300 × 75 ft on remnant ridge-and-trough topography

Type site for the Wando Series — the first formal description of a limestone-tempered pottery tradition restricted to the Wando River basin. Located on a long thin marsh island with Crevasse-Dawhoo soils, the most favorable fishing and shellfishing location on the tract. Of 32 sherds, 25 are limestone-tempered Wando ware (cord marked, check stamped). Temper confirmed as limestone (not shell) via HCl acid test and binocular microscope by Dr. Vincas Steponaitis — abundant rounded particles 0.5–6mm with near-surface leaching leaving characteristic rounded holes. Wando pottery is distinct from shell-tempered Oak Island ware: darker color (10YR3/2 to 7.5YR4/2), harder (Mohs 3–3.5 vs 2.5), and geographically restricted to the Wando drainage. Possible limestone sources include the Duplin formation (Cooper River/Goose Creek), Santee Limestone (Lake Marion bluffs), and Cooper Marl. Intact midden with both midden and non-midden areas — potential for intra-site spatial patterning.

Sources: Adams & Trinkley 1993, Chicora Foundation Research Series 35; Steponaitis (pers. comm.)

38CH1475 — Seaside Farms Marsh Island Midden

Woodland period — Wando (inferred from proximity to type site 38CH1474)

Location: Marsh island, Seaside Farms tract, Charleston County (32.802°N, 79.820°W)

National Register: Potentially Eligible — 200 × 50 ft

Intact shell midden located on the same long thin marsh island as 38CH1474 (Wando Series type site), approximately 200 ft to the east. Three of six shovel tests yielded shell midden deposits on Crevasse-Dawhoo ridge-and-trough topography. No diagnostic artifacts recovered during survey testing, but cultural context inferred from proximity to the type site. May represent a spatial extension of 38CH1474 or a related occupation area.

Sources: Adams & Trinkley 1993, Chicora Foundation Research Series 35

38CH177 — Causeway Midden (Destroyed)

Middle Woodland — Documented 1974, destroyed by erosion/collection

Location: End of causeway to marsh island, Seaside Farms tract, Charleston County (32.804°N, 79.817°W)

National Register: Not Eligible (heavily disturbed)

Remnant (~50 × 50 ft) of a Middle Woodland shell midden on a marsh hummock at the end of a causeway. Originally identified in 1974 by Trinkley & Carter with oyster and clam midden containing Middle Woodland sherds in tree falls. Revisited by Cabaniss in 1977. By 1992, no artifacts could be recovered — the site had been eroded or collected away. Documents the broader pattern of Woodland occupation on the Seaside Farms tract, where groups exploited marsh-edge environments across multiple islands and hummocks.

Sources: Trinkley & Carter 1974; Trinkley 1978b, 1980d; Adams & Trinkley 1993

Wando Territory

Extent: Wando River drainage — tidal marshes, barrier islands, and 'sea shore' lands of Christ Church Parish

Time: ~1000 BCE – early 1700s CE (Woodland through Contact periods)

People: Wando — small Muskhogean-speaking coastal group, part of the broader Cusabo alliance. Occupied the Wando River estuary, Inlet Creek, Swinton Creek, and surrounding marshlands.

Archaeology: Woodland period shell middens at Seaside Farms document occupation from Deptford through Santee/McClellanville phases. 38CH1474 is the type site for Wando Series limestone-tempered pottery — a ceramic tradition restricted to this drainage, distinct from Oak Island shell-tempered ware.

Contact: English settlement at Oyster Point (1680) placed colonists directly adjacent to Wando territory. Disease, the Indian slave trade, and displacement eliminated the Wando as a distinct polity by the early 1700s.

Sources: Adams & Trinkley 1993; Waddell 1980; Mooney 1894

Kinloch Site (38CH109)

Ancestral Cusabo — Late Archaic through Late Woodland (~2000 BCE – AD 1000)

Location: Near Savage Road / US 17 intersection, West Ashley, Charleston County (32.820°N, 80.040°W)

Multi-period Indigenous habitation site on a low sandy rise near a tributary of the Stono River, in the live oak forest strand biotope at the ecotone between the Coastal and Pine Barrens biomes — a strategically advantageous position for accessing resources from both ecological zones. Two prehistoric components span roughly 3,000 years of occupation. The earlier component (~2000–1000 BCE) yielded fiber tempered pottery, Thom's Creek / Awendaw Ware sherds, and a perforated baked clay object comparable to specimens from Charles Towne Landing (South 1971). The later component (~AD 500–1000) produced Wilmington Fabric-marked and possible Cape Fear Ware sherds. Together these represent the earliest and latest extremes of what Milanich (1972, 1973) defined as the Coastal Tradition — a continuous adaptation to the biotic resources of the Southeastern coastal plain spanning the Thom's Creek through Savannah cultural phases.

The non-shell midden character of the site was significant for understanding inland settlement patterns complementary to better-known shell ring and shell midden sites along the coast. Presence of pottery and baked clay objects suggested permanent habitation and whole-kin group activity — the authors hypothesized possible house structures similar to a Deptford house on Cumberland Island (Milanich 1972). The site also produced dense 18th century Colonial material including numerous Colono-Indian Ware sherds — evidence of continued Indigenous ceramic production and trade during the plantation era.

Sources: House & Goodyear 1975 (RMS 83); Carrillo 1980; Parler 1972 (SCIAA site files); Milanich 1972, 1973

Dog Pound Site (38CH262)

Ancestral Cusabo / Colonial Contact — Late Prehistoric through 18th century

Location: Former Charleston County Prison Farm, near Ashley River, Charleston County (32.840°N, 80.015°W)

Multi-component site on a high, well-drained rise beside a tidal creek flowing into the Ashley River. A sparse prehistoric component — two sand tempered pottery sherds and a heat-treated chert biface thinning flake — attests to Indigenous use of this elevated landform prior to European colonization. The primary component is a mid-18th century farmstead. Eleven Colono-Indian Ware sherds demonstrate the continued role of Indigenous ceramic traditions in Colonial society — these hand-built, low-fired earthenwares were produced by Native peoples and traded to or used alongside European-made goods. The presence of Colono-Indian Ware at plantation sites is central to understanding Indigenous-Colonial economic relationships in the 18th century Lowcountry.

Sources: House & Goodyear 1975 (RMS 83); SCIAA site files
